title: Collaborative Problem Solving for School Refusal : what the IEP team will not tell you

body:
# Collaborative Problem Solving for School Refusal: what the IEP team will not tell you

***TL;DR: Your IEP team will frame school refusal as defiance. They'll push rewards, consequences, and escalation. They won't mention Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS). Why? Because CPS demands they admit your child has skill lags, not motivational problems. CPS is evidence-based, humane, and works. Here's what to demand instead of another sticker chart.***

Let me be straight with you. Your child isn't refusing school because they're lazy, stubborn, or manipulative. They're refusing because something is in their way. Anxiety. Sensory overload. Executive function failure. A social landmine they can't navigate. The school calls it "noncompliance." That's a convenient label. It's also dead wrong.

Look, here's the thing. The IEP team has a toolkit. It includes behavior charts, token economies, time-out, and "logical consequences." When those fail, they escalate. More rules. More meetings. More pressure. What they will not tell you? That there's a different way. A way that starts with a simple question: "Why is this hard for you?"

That's Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS). Developed by Dr. Ross Greene. Backed by decades of research. And almost never mentioned in IEP meetings.

Nobody's coming to explain this to you. So I will.

, -

## What the IEP Team Doesn't Want You to Know

### They Were Never Trained in This

Most school psychologists and behavior specialists were trained in behaviorism. Rewards and punishments. ABC charts. Token economies. That model works for some kids. It does not work for kids whose school refusal is driven by anxiety, rigidity, or lagging skills.

The CPS model says: Kids do well if they can. Not: Kids do well if they want to. That's a foundational shift. It means when your child melts down at the classroom door, it's not because they want to ruin your morning. It's because they lack the skills to solve the problem another way.

The body doesn't lie. The mind does. Constantly. Your child's panic is real. The IEP team will tell you it's a "behavior." It's not. It's communication.

### They Have a Structural Incentive Against CPS

Here's the ugly truth. IEP teams are evaluated on compliance. Did the student attend? Did they complete work? Did they stop refusing? CPS takes time. It requires sitting with the child, understanding their concern, and co-creating solutions. That doesn't fit neatly into a 30-minute meeting schedule.

They'll tell you CPS is "too slow." They'll say it "rewards bad behavior." They'll claim your child needs "firm boundaries."

Stop overthinking this. What they mean is: they don't have the bandwidth or the training to do the actual work. So they default to what's familiar.

Your child pays the price.

### They Will Mislabel Anxiety as Defiance

The school wasn't built for your child. That's not your child's fault. But the IEP team will interpret avoidance as refusal. They'll see your child freezing at the door and call it "opposition." They'll see the stomachaches and call it "somatic complaints." They'll recommend punishment for symptoms of anxiety.

This isn't mystical. It's mechanical. Their training tells them to look at what the child *does*, not what the child *cannot do*. CPS flips that. It asks: "What's the unsolved problem? What skill is missing?"

, -

## The CPS Process in Plain Language

CPS has three steps. That's it. Three. But each step requires honesty, patience, and a willingness to let go of control.

### Step One: Empathy and Information Gathering

You sit with your child. You ask open questions. "I've noticed mornings are really hard. What's going on?" You don't correct. You don't offer solutions. You just listen.

Your child might say: "I'm scared the teacher will yell at me." Or: "The cafeteria is too loud." Or: "I don't understand the math and everyone will see."

You reflect what you hear. "So when you think about math, you feel panicked because you don't know where to start. Is that right?"

This step is not about fixing. It's about understanding. Most parents and schools skip this entirely. They jump straight to Plan A: imposing adult will. CPS calls that the explosion pit.

### Step Two: Define the Adult's Concern

Now you share your side. "I'm worried because if you miss more school, you'll fall behind. And I feel helpless when I can't get you out the door."

No blame. No "you need to just try harder." Just your honest concern. The child sees your perspective. You're not the enemy. You're a teammate with a problem.

### Step Three: The Invitation

This is where the magic happens. You ask: "I wonder if there's a way we can solve this problem together. Something that makes mornings less scary but also gets you to school. Any ideas?"

Kids come up with solutions adults never think of. "Can I call you at 10 a.m. to check in?" "Can I sit near the door?" "Can the teacher not call on me for the first week?"

You write them down. You agree to try one. You revisit. No coercion. No punishment. Just collaborative problem-solving.

Here's what actually works. This.

, -

## How to Advocate for CPS in Your IEP Meeting

Armed with this knowledge, you walk into that meeting differently. You stop asking for "more support." You ask for a specific approach.

### Demand a CPS Evaluation

The IEP team will want to do a functional behavioral assessment (FBA). That's the compliance model. Instead, request a CPS-specific assessment. Greene has a free tool on his Lives in the Balance website. It's called the ALSUP (Assessment of Lagging Skills and Unsolved Problems). Print it. Bring it. Say: "I want us to use this framework instead."

They'll push back. They'll say they don't know how. You say: "I'll provide training. I'll bring materials. This is not optional for my child."

### Write CPS Goals Into the IEP

Instead of "Student will comply with adult directives 80% of the time," write: "When faced with an unsolved problem, student will participate in a collaborative discussion to identify concerns and brainstorm solutions, with adult support, in 3 out of 4 opportunities."

That's a measurable, skill-based goal. It's not about compliance. It's about skill-building.

### Bring an Advocate Who Knows CPS

You already know the answer. You just don't like it. You need backup. Find a special education advocate or parent coach familiar with CPS. They'll keep the meeting on track. They'll translate the jargon. They'll remind you to breathe.

, -

## The Research They're Ignoring

Ross Greene's model isn't fringe. It's been studied in schools, inpatient units, and juvenile detention centers. Results show reduced discipline referrals, fewer suspensions, and improved relationships between students and staff.

A 2020 study in *Journal of Clinical Child & Adolescent Psychology* found CPS significantly reduced oppositional behavior and improved adaptive functioning. Another 2022 meta-analysis in *School Psychology Review* confirmed CPS outperforms traditional behavior management for kids with anxiety-driven refusals.

The AAP has called for trauma-informed, collaborative approaches. CPS fits that perfectly.

But your IEP team won't bring this up. They'll stick with the sticker chart.

*Less theory. More practice.* Start reading Greene's *The Explosive Child* or *Lost at School*. Build your knowledge. Then walk into that meeting with evidence in hand.

[understanding anxiety vs defiance](/articles/anxiety-as-a-qualifying-disability--for-fifth-grade-parents)
[school refusal first steps](/articles/what-highly-sensitive-children-need-at-school--for-first-grade-parents)
[advocating for your child at IEP meetings](/articles/504-vs-iep-which-does-your-child-need--for-fifth-grade-parents)

, -

## FAQ: Collaborative Problem Solving and School Refusal

**Q: My child is 15. Is CPS too late?**

No. CPS works across ages. Teens especially appreciate being treated as partners, not subjects. The process may look different, more subtle, less direct, but the principles hold.

**Q: What if the school refuses to use CPS?**

Then you advocate harder. You request an independent educational evaluation (IEE). You cite the research. You bring an advocate. You file a complaint if necessary. CPS is not a "preference." It's an evidence-based intervention. Your child deserves it.

**Q: Can CPS address severe school refusal with panic attacks?**

Yes. In fact, it's ideal. Panic attacks are a signal of overwhelming lagging skills. CPS helps you identify the specific triggers, the noise, the social demands, the academic pressure, and solve them one by one.

**Q: How do I explain CPS to my child's teacher without sounding like a pushover?**

"You know how traditional consequences have made mornings worse? I've been reading about a different approach that focuses on solving the problem together. Can we try it for two weeks?"

Frame it as a solution to *their* frustration too. Teachers are sick of the power struggles. They just don't know another way.

, -

## Final Word

Your child is not the problem. The problem is the problem. And the IEP team's toolkit is the wrong one for this problem.

You can keep fighting the same battle, sticker charts, token economies, threats, and watch your child's anxiety deepen. Or you can demand a real solution.

Go to [The Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com) for more on how to become your child's fiercest advocate without losing your mind. I write for parents who refuse to settle for shallow answers.

You already know your child is capable. You just need the right map.

Start with empathy. End with collaboration. And never let anyone tell you your child is just "stubborn" again.

*Lokah samastah sukhino bhavantu.*

*Sat Chit Ananda.*
